text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic hernia repair by identifying the course of the round ligament of the uterus, searching for adhesions that may distort the region. A femoral defect is found alongside the right external iliac vein, medial ligament, and round ligament. The medial border of the external iliac vein is visualized, intersecting with the round ligament, marking the minimal level for dissection of the peritoneum flap downwards.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and begins dissection of the preperitoneal space, identifying Cooper's ligament and the femoral defect with a narrow orifice and dense adhesions. After widening the orifice, the hernia content is reduced, and the defect margins are cleared. A lower peritoneal flap is formed to develop space for a synthetic mesh, ensuring the flap is wide enough to cover the mesh without lifting it. The round ligament of the uterus, which thickens and enters the uterine horn, is dissected due to its obstruction in females.

The surgeon attempts to separate the round ligament from the peritoneum near the uterine horn using scissors and coagulation. As the peritoneum becomes thin, blunt dissection is employed to separate the structures, resulting in minor bleeding that is controlled with coagulation.

The surgeon lifts the peritoneal flap to gain a better perspective of the uterine horn's location. The peritoneum remains intact as the surgeon attempts to sweep it off the round ligament. During blunt dissection, minor bleeding occurs, which is controlled with coagulation. A small tear is visible in the peritoneal flap upon inspection.

The surgeon observes a small rent in the peritoneum from the upper side, which facilitates dissection by allowing better control of the round ligament and uterus position. The round ligament is released sufficiently, and a synthetic mesh is placed without interference between its lower margin and the round ligament.

The surgeon fixes the mesh to Cooper's ligament with a single suture, leaving the needle inside the peritoneal cavity for later use. The peritoneum is then closed tightly with a V-lock suture, separating the mesh from the peritoneal cavity, though contact remains at the proximal part of the round ligament. The surgeon retrieves the needle and closes the defect above the torn peritoneum with a single Z-type suture, ensuring a tight closure to prevent bowel herniation into the preperitoneal space or mesh.

The surgeon performs a final inspection, confirming the complete closure of the peritoneum and the proper positioning of the mesh. The mesh is observed to be flat with no folding, ensuring there is no risk of internal herniation at the sutured locations.

The surgeon opens the preperitoneal space in a 42-year-old female with a right indirect hernia. The round ligament is visible through the peritoneum. The Cooper's ligament is identified, and dissection of the hernia sac from the inguinal canal begins.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the round ligament, noting that the ligament appears thicker and more fragile while the peritoneum becomes thinner. The two structures adhere tightly, resembling a merged entity, increasing the risk of incidental peritoneal opening.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum parallel to the medial border of the round ligament, extending the incision towards the uterine horn. The dissection line reaches close to the uterus. A similar incision is made on the lateral side of the round ligament, using scissors to cut downwards to match the medial side's level. This ensures adequate mesh overlap, extending beyond the medial border of the external iliac vein.

The surgeon inserts and unfolds the mesh, ensuring the lower margin does not touch the edge of the dissected peritoneal flap. The peritoneum incision is closed using an absorbable V-lock suture, leaving the excess thread uncut. After invaginating the hernia sac, two defects in the peritoneum are identified. The smaller defect is closed with a few suture passes between the peritoneum and hernia sac, allowing the surgeon to placate the hernia sac and reach the apex of the lower defect.

The surgeon tightly closes the rent in the peritoneum with a locked suture, preventing internal herniation. A suction tube is inserted beyond the peritoneum, and gas deflation is performed, confirming the appropriate closure and mesh position.
